repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
I mentioned this patch in the bug #5652, but realised it should be a PR.

Vim ignores `:lmap` when executing a mapped command as documented in `:help language-mapping`, but this messes up macro replay.

Example:
```
:set iminsert=1<CR>
:lnoremap a l<CR>
qaiaaa<CR><Esc>q@a
```
which inserts two lines: `lll` and `aaa`.

This extra line adds an exception for macro replay.

comment_data.body: in general use command() instead of feed_command() unless the asynchrony is intended. Also feed_command() doesn't bubble errors up (though it does set v:errmsg, but that needs to be checked explicitly)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -126,3 +128,208 @@ describe('input utf sequences that contain CSI/K_SPECIAL', function()
     expect('…')
   end)
 end)
+
+
+-- Testing 'keymap'.
+-- First test it's implemented using the :lmap and :lnoremap commands, then
+-- check those mappings behave as expected.
+describe("'keymap' / :lmap", function()
+  clear()
+  before_each(function()
+    clear()
+    insert("lllaaa")
+    feed_command('set iminsert=1')
+    feed_command('set imsearch=1')
+    feed_command('lmap l a')
+    feed('gg0')
+  end)
+
+  describe("'keymap' as :lnoremap", function()
+    before_each(function()
+      feed_command('lmapclear')
+      feed_command('lmapclear <buffer>')
+      feed_command('set keymap=dvorak')
+    end)
+
+    -- Shows that 'keymap' sets language mappings without allowing
+    -- remapping of those mappings.
+    -- This equivalence allows us to only test :lmap commands and assert
+    -- they behave the same as 'keymap' settings.
+    -- It does rely on the absence of special code that implements 'keymap'
+    -- and :lmap differently but shows mappings from the 'keymap' after
+    -- typing :lmap.
+    it("'keymap' mappings are shown with :lmap", function()
+      feed_command('set nomore')
+      local bindings = funcs.nvim_command_output('lmap')
+      eq(bindings, dedent([[
+
+      l  "           *@_
+      l  '           *@-
+      l  +           *@}
+      l  ,           *@w
+      l  -           *@[
+      l  .           *@v
+      l  /           *@z
+      l  :           *@S
+      l  ;           *@s
+      l  <           *@W
+      l  =           *@]
+      l  >           *@V
+      l  ?           *@Z
+      l  A           *@A
+      l  B           *@X
+      l  C           *@J
+      l  D           *@E
+      l  E           *@>
+      l  F           *@U
+      l  G           *@I
+      l  H           *@D
+      l  I           *@C
+      l  J           *@H
+      l  K           *@T
+      l  L           *@N
+      l  M           *@M
+      l  N           *@B
+      l  O           *@R
+      l  P           *@L
+      l  Q           *@"
+      l  R           *@P
+      l  S           *@O
+      l  T           *@Y
+      l  U           *@G
+      l  V           *@K
+      l  W           *@<
+      l  X           *@Q
+      l  Y           *@F
+      l  Z           *@:
+      l  [           *@/
+      l  \           *@\
+      l  ]           *@=
+      l  _           *@{
+      l  a           *@a
+      l  b           *@x
+      l  c           *@j
+      l  d           *@e
+      l  e           *@.
+      l  f           *@u
+      l  g           *@i
+      l  h           *@d
+      l  i           *@c
+      l  j           *@h
+      l  k           *@t
+      l  l           *@n
+      l  m           *@m
+      l  n           *@b
+      l  o           *@r
+      l  p           *@l
+      l  q           *@'
+      l  r           *@p
+      l  s           *@o
+      l  t           *@y
+      l  u           *@g
+      l  v           *@k
+      l  w           *@,
+      l  x           *@q
+      l  y           *@f
+      l  z           *@;
+      l  {           *@?
+      l  |           *@|
+      l  }           *@+]]))
+    end)
+    it("'keymap' mappings are not remappable", function()
+    end)
+  end)
+  describe("'iminsert' option", function()
+    it("Uses :lmap in insert mode when ON", function()
+      feed('il<esc>')
+      expect('alllaaa')
+    end)
+    it("Ignores :lmap in insert mode when OFF", function()
+      feed_command('set iminsert=0')
+      feed('il<esc>')
+      expect('llllaaa')
+    end)
+    it("Can be toggled with <C-^> in insert mode", function()
+      feed('i<C-^>l<C-^>l<esc>')
+      expect('lalllaaa')
+      eq(eval('&iminsert'), 1)
+      feed('i<C-^><esc>')
+      eq(eval('&iminsert'), 0)
+    end)
+  end)
+  describe("'imsearch' option", function()
+    it("Uses :lmap at search prompt when ON", function()
+      feed('/lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('lll')
+    end)
+    it("Ignores :lmap at search prompt when OFF", function()
+      feed_command('set imsearch=0')
+      feed('gg/lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('aaa')
+    end)
+    it("Can be toggled with C-^", function()
+      eq(eval('&imsearch'), 1)
+      feed('/<C-^>lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('aaa')
+      eq(eval('&imsearch'), 0)
+      feed('u0/<C-^>lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('lll')
+      eq(eval('&imsearch'), 1)
+    end)
+    it("can follow 'iminsert'", function()
+      feed_command('set imsearch=-1')
+      feed('/lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('lll')
+      eq(eval('&imsearch'), -1)
+      eq(eval('&iminsert'), 1)
+      feed('u/<C-^>lll<cr>3x')
+      expect('aaa')
+      eq(eval('&imsearch'), -1)
+      eq(eval('&iminsert'), 0)
+    end)
+  end)
+  it(":lmap not applied to macros", function()
+    command("call setreg('a', 'il')")
+    feed('@a')
+    expect('llllaaa')
+    eq(call('getreg', 'a'), 'il')
+  end)
+  it(":lmap applied to macro recording", function()
+    feed('qail<esc>q@a')
+    expect('aalllaaa')
+    eq(call('getreg', 'a'), 'ia')
+  end)
+  it(":lmap not applied to mappings", function()
+    feed_command('imap t l')
+    feed('it<esc>')
+    expect('llllaaa')
+  end)
+  it("mappings applied to keys created with :lmap", function()
+    feed_command('imap a x')

comment_data.body:
Let's put this in `test/functional/options/keymap_spec.lua`. 

(`test/functional/ui/...` is for testing the UI)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -126,3 +128,208 @@ describe('input utf sequences that contain CSI/K_SPECIAL', function()
     expect('…')
   end)
 end)
+
+
+-- Testing 'keymap'.

comment_data.body: Usually `len = -1` is used for this purpose. Any reason it wasn't here?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1087,18 +1087,28 @@ void del_typebuf(int len, int offset)
 /*
  * Write typed characters to script file.
  * If recording is on put the character in the recordbuffer.
+ * if `null_terminated` is true, then ignore `len` and continue until reach the
+ * NULL character in `chars`.

comment_data.body: I see one new test for checking that the change works with macros, but are there any risks of regression that you can think of (to test against)?

Other than testing basic macros I can't think of any.
I had assumed there were tests for macros already in the testsuite, but I can't find them at the moment, so I'm adding some to this PR.
If len == -1, write all characters in NUL-terminated `chars`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1087,18 +1087,28 @@ void del_typebuf(int len, int offset)
 /*
  * Write typed characters to script file.
  * If recording is on put the character in the recordbuffer.
+ * if len == -1, then write all characters in the NULL terminated `chars`.

comment_data.body:
why can't this be:

    gotchars(s, strlen(s));

I thought you might want to avoid the double iteration over characters in the expansion.

i.e. we already have a loop iterating over all characters in the buffer, so counting the length with `strlen()` is effectively iterating over the characters twice when it's not needed.

Honestly I wasn't sure which to use -- I guessed which would be preferred by others -- I'm happy to change it if you'd like.
Ah, good point. But LANGMAP isn't a common case, so better to keep it simple.

No problem.

Just to note: for me LANGMAP is a very common case (~30% of keys in insert mode), but the map expansions are all one character long so it's still not a performance problem :-)

comment_regular_data.body:
If I recall, this has come up on vim_dev recently (couldn't find the recent discussion, but [here](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/vim_dev/vuEmHFB7ERM/dFX72eiH32UJ) is an older one). Bram always rejects it. Should we break Vim-compat here? Why? What are the alternatives? Can you comment on that discussion (if it's relevant, I didn't look closely).

Maybe @chrisbra can comment. Also, did [this discussion](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/vim_dev/QnNwLWhJ744/1qNcD7d9OvgJ) ever get fully resolved? (Is it relevant? I didn't look closely.)

cc @ZyX-I @blueyed

I believe the discussion you are refering to has been merged as the 'langnoremap' option. However there have been a couple of more discussions, e.g. vim/vim#543. The problem is, this part of the code seems extremly fragile, changing something usually breaks in subtile ways, so one needs to have propper tests for those kind of changes.

As if to prove @chrisbra 's point of it being fragile, I noticed that my change added in some bad behaviour when a macro recording includes an `:nmap` that inserts some text including some `:lmap`ped characters.

```
:nmap l ix
:lmap x q
qalll<CR><Esc>q@a
```
You see one line of `xll` and another of `qll` with my patch, and two lines of `xll` beforehand, so what I have suggested here is broken.


Reading the discussion of  vim/vim#543 (thanks for pointing to that) I think the changes that are discussed there sound much better than the patch I gave here. Bram says

> I look at keymap/langmap as a low level operation, which is applied when
> typing on the keyboard, before it goes to the rest of Vim, which is
> unaware of this filtering.

which would work with recording a macro and trying to replay it (unlike the current behaviour), and make things easier to understand than now.
Also, the discussion there of applying `'langmap'` to cmdline mode seems sensible too.


As a side note: it seems that starting nvim with `nvim -u NONE` means that while `:set langnoremap?` says the setting is on, the effect isn't seen.

I'll try and comprehensibly write down the current behaviour and where it differs from the mental model of a conversion between the user typed characters and Vim itself, so we can see what other changes that simpler model would add. I'll also have a look at the patch in vim/vim#543 to see if that patch makes this change.

My opinion (though I'm biased because the current behaviour annoys me) is that
the current behaviour is too difficult to reason about, and in this specific
instance (macros and keymap) essentially blocks macros from being used.
At the moment, the only way to run a macro with characters you don't have on
the keyboard but have translated in the `keymap` is to manually put the text
into that register instead of recording it interactively, which is enough of a
pain that I don't do it.

After thinking more on it I would actually propose a different solution to my
problem: instead of applying `:lmap` mappings to the register when executing
it, I would apply them when interactively recording into the register (I
haven't yet checked whether this would be an easy change or not).
Assuming other users of `keymap` also don't record macros interactively because
of this difficulty, then this wouldn't break anyones workflow, and would make
macros with `keymap` much more usable.


Assuming the settings `langnoremap` and using `:lmap` then there are only
really two places that the mental model of a pre-translation of characters is
broken. One is what happens during macro recording that is the original problem
above, and the other is in the priority that mappings between `:lmap` and
`:imap` are applied.
Instead of `:lmap` getting priority (as it would be if the mental model would
hold, it depends on the order the mappings are defined).

Example:

```
:set iminsert=1
:lmap a l
:imap a i
oaaa
```
Inserts `iii` -- i.e. the `imap` mappings are applied first.


Although this is be dependant on the order the mappings were defined
Example:
```
:set iminsert=1
:imap a i
:lmap a l
oaaa
```
Inserts `lll` -- i.e. the `lmap` mappings are applied first.


I think this should be different, but frankly it bothers me much less, so I
don't mind if not.


I agree there should be thorough testing here -- it's been pretty difficult
for me to understand what's going on sometimes. I'll be happy to start on that.

In the meantime I'll have a look at what changes the vim/vim#543 PR introduces to see if there's anything interesting there.

> instead of applying :lmap mappings to the register when executing it, I would apply them when interactively recording into the register

But then the input from the macro (during replay) is not respecting `keymap` or `langmap`? Which is correct, based on Bram's comments in https://github.com/vim/vim/pull/543. 

> The 'keymap' option is meant for when typing text in Insert mode. It
> does not apply to when typing commands, in Normal mode. The 'langmap'
> option is the other way around.
> 
> A register contains commands, which may start Insert mode and then
> insert some text. Thus it's a mix. Translating all characters with
> 'keymap' (or 'langmap') will cause problems.
>
> I wonder how you managed to get characters into a register that you want
> to apply 'keymap' or 'langmap' to. **This translation should have already
> happened when typing the characters.**

And later:

> Perhaps the problem is that when recording into
> a register the keymap/langmap is not applied, resulting in the wrong
> characters in the register?

> I look at keymap/langmap as a low level operation, which is applied when
> typing on the keyboard, before it goes to the rest of Vim, which is
> unaware of this filtering.

@hardenedapple so I think you're right, the keys should be translated before being stored in the macro register. Based on Bram's comments, it is already expected to do so.

And using the Neovim test infrastructure it should be easy to correctly test this. (`helpers.feed()` is identical to raw user input)

I believe this fixes most of the problems with `:lmap`, `'keymap'`, and `'langmap'` while still keeping the implementation essentially the same.

~~I intend to try and write a completely new implementation, but seeing as that will probably have backwards compatibility problems I'll keep that for a different PR.~~
It turned out my alternate implementation isn't any better at all.

Making `'keymap'` respect the mental model of a translation before getting to vim proper was a reasonably clean and simple task.
Making `'langmap'` respect the mental model is much more awkward, and I would like some input on what we should aim for.

Hence I've made this PR just fix the `'keymap'` / `:lmap` discrepancies, and will be opening another PR to show and start working on the `'langmap'` behaviour.
LGTM, let's merge it soon after 0.2.
@hardenedapple would you mind rebasing this to get an updated CI run?

@justinmk I just realised I should ping you after doing the rebase you asked for.
(I guess there's no other way you could tell I did it).

@hardenedapple Do we need to update any docs here? Or at least a sentence in vim_diff.txt.

Also does this close any issues?

@justinmk I've added mention in the docs, thanks for the suggestion :-p .

I have `close #5652` as the second line of the commit message in b8c35f5942d38888312972c6a976f10316c31301, that's good for the automation to pick up right?

In [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/6834#issuecomment-305770354) @aeruder mentioned a negative implication of applying maps before recording.

That issue affects the changes I have here, mappings that act on keys that come from `:lnoremap` are applied during macro replay but not macro recording.

I'm switching the PR to `[WIP]` until I can think of another approach.
At the moment the best thing I can think of is to change the `buffheader_T` structure to include a member equivalent to the `tb_noremap` in a `typebuf_T` structure, but this would be a lot of change for very little benefit, so I don't like it.
Is this still WIP? Are there parts that can be merged?

@justinmk Sorry about the stale PR.
I'm going to reply from memory, so forgive statements like "the macro replay
mechanism" instead of mentioning the specific functions & data structures.

In reply to your question:
As discussed above, the aim of this PR was to make it so that when recording a
macro using mappings from `:lmap`, the action that was affected during
recording would be the same action affected during replay.

This works, and I personally use this, but in the current state it introduces
a problem as it means that macro replay of keys that used `:lnoremap` would
have mappings applied to them, while that wouldn't happen during macro
recording.

The reason this happens is pretty core to the implementation of macros and
`:lmap`.
In most places the `:lmap` mappings can be thought of as a translation that
happens *before* the rest of vim proper gets to work.
Yanking some commands into a register and then replaying those commands should
not (and does not) apply `:lmap` translations (as the comments in
https://github.com/vim/vim/pull/543 seem to agree with).

The implementation of this PR is that when recording a macro, the keys stored
in the macro buffer are now the keys after translation by `:lmap` instead of
what keys were pressed.
Hence the replay acts the same, but what is stored is different.

This causes the with `:lnoremap` because the macro replay mechanism has no way
to say "Don't map these keys.".

To my sensibilities this is a perfectly good tradeoff: `:lnoremap` doesn't make
sense to me, as it's mixing mental models.
The `:lmap` mental model is that the majority of vim doesn't know that the
translation has been made, but if that's so then it shouldn't be able to
decide not to apply any more mappings after that.
(In fact, in the branch I work on day-to-day I have completely disabled
`:lnoremap`).

On the other hand (and the reason I put this PR into `[WIP]`), it's a backwards
incompatilibity that I have no way of telling whether it would be bad for the
majority of users.
I use the `:lmap` stuff as a hack rather than how it was intended, so I don't
feel able to decide if this is a valid tradeoff for everyone.

I've played around with some thoughts about what to do, and as yet the only
feasible method of getting the functionality this PR is intended to introduce
without adding some backwards incompatibility is the modification to the
`buffheader_T` structure I mentioned above.
i.e. store something similar to what the `typebuf` does in order to determine
which keys to map and which not to.

I don't like that as an option, so I haven't really done anything about it.

Something that's just come to mind now -- so I'm working without the details being fresh -- is that I might be able to only apply the translation when using `:lmap`, but in that case this PR doesn't affect `'keymap'` which I believe is the majority of the use of `:lmap/:lnoremap`.

I can't see any resolution in the near future, so I can close the PR if you
want.

@hardenedapple I think it's good to avoid a new field on bufheader_T. 

If there's an advantage to breaking compatibility here that we can clearly explain to users, we could just try it and see who complains.  So if you think it's ready (needs a rebase at least) we can do that.

I have rebased the changes onto the current master, and checked that things still work locally.

Besides the stated aim of getting replay of a macro recorded while :lmap is active to produce the same actions when replaying as when it was recorded, there are two other things to mention.

### 'keymap' uses :lmap
I have made 'keymap' use :lmap instead of :lnoremap because that way users can
use :imap with it and the macro situation is correct for 'keymap'.

From a comment in https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/vim_dev/vuEmHFB7ERM/dFX72eiH32UJ it seems like 'keymap' was using :lnoremap in order to avoid introducing
backwards incompatibility (the explanation comes up at the end of the thread,
after a suggestion about it being to do with avoiding map loops gets raised
and then corrected). 
Seeing as we're already breaking backwards compatibility in this area, and
the point is to make macros and remapping "just work" I thought this the sensible choice.
With this change in, leaving 'keymap' as using :lnoremap means a more broken behaviour
around macros for users of 'keymap', while switching to using :lmap means introducing a
more consistent behaviour with :imap *and* behaving as expected with macros.

### :lnoremap behaviour
Due to the switch of 'keymap' from :lnoremap to :lmap I saw three options of how to deal
with the direct :lnoremap command
- Disable it, and warn users -- so that no-one ends up with strange behaviour that's difficult to diagnose.
- Leave :lnoremap alone, letting users still have the functionality but possibly introducing confusing situations if they use :lnoremap to influence the behaviour of 'keymap' (my preferred option as this seems like an unlikely case).
- Make :lnoremap behave just as :lmap, which means the influence of :lnoremap on 'keymap' will be the same as before, but the behaviour of :lnoremap would not be as expected.

> Leave :lnoremap alone, letting users still have the functionality but possibly introducing confusing situations if they use :lnoremap to influence the behaviour of 'keymap' (my preferred option as this seems like an unlikely case).

Let's try that, and revisit if needed.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and agility.
repo.name: neovim

title: apply `:lmap` in macros
